coredata.dc:title: Antimalarial nano-drug delivery system based on graphene quantum dot on Plasmodium falciparum: Preparation, characterization, toxicological evaluation
coredata.dc:creator: Torkashvand, Hossein
Dehdast, S. Ahmad
Nateghpour, Mehdi
Haghi, Afsaneh Motevalli
Fard, Ghazaleh Chizari
Elmi, Taher
Shabani, Mohammad
Tabatabaie, Fatemeh
coredata.dc:description: Malaria infects millions of people every year. Emerging drug resistance in many strains of Plasmodium falciparum against the currently used antimalarial is one of the major obstacles in the way of elimination of falciparum malaria infection. Quantum dot is a fluorescent nanocrystal that has been in the spotlight as a practical tool for the visualization of live cell processes and creating a nano-sized controlled release system. We tried to incorporated artesunate (Art), mefloquine (Mef) alone, chitosan (chi), and in combination (Art-Mef) form in the graphene quantum dot (GQD)-based nanocomposites to increase the antimalarial effect of the against. Release of the drugs became optimized by crosslinking the drugs with GQD and their eventual incorporation into the nanodrugs. Release of the drugs was also controlled loading on GQD. Results showed that the release of drugs were continued during 98 %, 96 % and 90 % (36 h) for GQD-Art/Chi, GQD-Mef/Chi and GQD-Art-Mef/Chi, respectively. The prepared nanodrugs had an IC50s of 9.2, 18.6 and 3.6 μg/mL for GQD-Art/Chi, GQD-Mef/Chi and GQD-Art-Mef/Chi, respectively. Cell culture study indicated that different concentrations of the preparation nano-drugs had not any toxicity against the PC12 cell line. It was concluded that the prepared nanodrugs had a suitable effect on the cultured P. falciparum in this study.

head.ce:title: Antimalarial nano-drug delivery system based on graphene quantum dot on Plasmodium falciparum: Preparation, characterization, toxicological evaluation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Malaria infects millions of people every year. Emerging drug resistance in many strains of Plasmodium falciparum against the currently used antimalarial is one of the major obstacles in the way of elimination of falciparum malaria infection. Quantum dot is a fluorescent nanocrystal that has been in the spotlight as a practical tool for the visualization of live cell processes and creating a nano-sized controlled release system. We tried to incorporated artesunate (Art), mefloquine (Mef) alone, chitosan (chi), and in combination (Art-Mef) form in the graphene quantum dot (GQD)-based nanocomposites to increase the antimalarial effect of the against. Release of the drugs became optimized by crosslinking the drugs with GQD and their eventual incorporation into the nanodrugs. Release of the drugs was also controlled loading on GQD. Results showed that the release of drugs were continued during 98 %, 96 % and 90 % (36 h) for GQD-Art/Chi, GQD-Mef/Chi and GQD-Art-Mef/Chi, respectively. The prepared nanodrugs had an IC50s of 9.2, 18.6 and 3.6 μg/mL for GQD-Art/Chi, GQD-Mef/Chi and GQD-Art-Mef/Chi, respectively. Cell culture study indicated that different concentrations of the preparation nano-drugs had not any toxicity against the PC12 cell line. It was concluded that the prepared nanodrugs had a suitable effect on the cultured P. falciparum in this study.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article.

ce:section.ce:para: The World Health Organization (WHO) defines malaria as one of the infectious diseases of poverty (DoP) with global health importance in tropical and subtropical regions [1]. The report of WHO was shown that the malaria cases in the three regions was in the range of 95.2 %, 2.5 %, and 1.6 % for the African, South-east Asian, and Eastern Mediterranean regions, respectively [2].

Nowadays, drugs such as artemisinin, artesunate, primaquine, mefloquine, and lomfantrin are used to treat malaria. These drugs not only, having many side effects, including anorexia, abdominal cramps, diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, and visual disturbances, but also are contraindicated in pregnant women, children, and people with G6PD deficiency [3,4]. The side effects of the mentioned drugs, on the one hand, and the development of drug resistance in P. falciparum and partially in P. vivax, on the other hand, have caused some problems in the use of these drugs to treat malaria infection. So, for finding novel and effective alternative drugs more studies would be implemented.

Nanoscience and nanotechnology have been well adapted to bring innovative solutions to the enormous challenge of winning the fight against DoP [5]. Nanomaterials, which have been extensively focused on cancer-related biomedical applications for many years, are being intensively investigated to implement strategies to prevent and cure malaria, with the aim of overcoming the problems arising from conventional therapies [6,7]. Many nanocarriers have the capacity to encapsulate different types of drugs and of being functionalized with ligands targeting them to the desired cell type. In addition, if the nanocarriers are loaded with large numbers of drug molecules to be delivered together, their antiparasitic effects will be potentiated and they will have a higher chance to kill quickly the infectious agent.

According to physical and mechanical properties of carrier, the drugs shows many different physical and biological response. Therefore, the nano-carrier play an important role to high efficiency of drug. Polymer nanoparticles/nano-capsules, micelles, microemulsion/nano-emulsion, quantum dots (QDs), and dendrimers are the main nano-carries [8]. As cited by Zhao et al. [9], quantum dots have a unique optical properties, which can be introduced as a potential candidates for nano-carries. Adsorption, dispersing, coupling, and dissolving are the main properties of QDs that be candidate for nano-carries [10].

Interestingly, the Amphotericin B nano-assemblies were more effective in extermination different fungal pathogens and educed less drug related toxic manifestations in the host as compared to free form of the drug [5]. Murkherjee et al. [11] reported that minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of tetracycline-loaded calcium phosphate nanoparticle (Tet-CPNP) on tetracycline resistant bacteria like Escherichia coli, Salmonella kentuckey, and Shigella flexneri was in the range of 20–40 μg/mL, whereas MIC of free tetracycline was in the range of 150–180 μg/mL. Therefore, studies proved the suspension containing these particles has a better antimicrobial effect, and that various drugs have less nephrotoxicity when used with nano-drug carriers. It has been reported the drug side effects can be reduced when used in conjunction with specific nano-carriers [5,11].

Artesunate and mefloquine are an anti-malaria medicines respectively that have been shown to have anticancer, antimicrobial, and anti-parasitic effects in recent years. It is also effective in preventing and treating malaria, but due to its drug side effects and lack of combination, it needs carriers for transmission [12–14]. Various researches have been employed to overcome this issue, one of which is nano-dendrimers with high solubility in water. So, with loading the drug combination of artesunate and mefloquine into this nanocomposite, its effectiveness on the Plasmodium parasite can be increased [15,16].

Oyeyemi et al. [17] reported that curcumin-artesunate based polymeric nanoparticle could be used as a simple method of dual entrapment of curcumin and artesunate with better antiplasmodial efficacy and low toxicity. Another study indicate Andrographis paniculata Nees (Acanthaceae) synthesized silver nanoparticle has an anti-plasmodial activity potential which can added valuable products from medicinal plants for biomedical and nanotechnology based industries [18]. Curcumin and artemisinin have the chemical interaction to show fungistatic and fungicidal properties especially on P. falciparum and Plasmodium berghei in culture. In vivo study indicated 3 oral doses of curcumin following a single injection of α,β-arteether to P. berghei-infected mice were able to prevent recrudescence due to α,β-arteether monotherapy and ensure almost 100 % survival of the animals [19].

The novelty of the present study can be summarized as I) the artesunate and mefloquine were not loaded on graphene quantum dot and its release behavior was not investigated up to now and II) the effect of this drug nano delivery systems as anti-malarial has not been evaluated up to now. In this study, artesunate and mefloquine were loaded on graphene quantum dot (GQD) and then the effect of nano drugs was investigated.

Sodium hydroxide, citric acid, methanol, ethanol and Giemsa stain were obtained from Merck. Artesunate (Art) (MW = 384.42, cat No. A3731), Mefloquine (Mef) (MW = 414.77, EC No. 257-412-0, MDL No. MFCD00797519), Gentamicin, sorbitol and Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Packed red blood cells O+ (PC), Fresh frozen plasma AB+ (FFP) were obtained from Blood Transfusion organization of Iran. Normal Saline Solution was purchased from Iranian parenteral and pharmaceutical co. RPMI 1640 Medium 1640 (1×) + GlutaMAX liquid was obtained from Gibco. Albumax were purchased from Vizcarra pharma (Vizcarra Pharmaceutical, Ermita, Manila, Philippines). The 3D7 strain of P. falciparum was obtained from Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQD and stabilization of artesunate and mefloquine
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation GQD and stabilization of artesunate and mefloquine on GQD was produced by a two-step method. First, the GQDs were prepared by directly pyrolyzing CA. In a typical procedure of GQDs preparation, 2 g citric acid (CA) was put into a 5 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C using a heating mantle. About 5 min later, the CA was liquated. Subsequently, the colour of the liquid was changed from colourless to pale yellow, and then orange in 30 min, implying the formation of GQDs. The obtained orange liquid for preparing GQDs was added drop by drop into 100 mL of 10 mg mL−1 NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring. After neutralizing to pH 7.0 with NaOH, the aqueous solution of GQDs was obtained. Second, different amounts (X = 5, 10, 20 and 30 mg) of mefloquine were added into the GQD solution with vigorously stirring at different temperatures (−20, 24, 37 and 60 °C) for 60 min. Third, different amounts (X = 5, 10, 20 and 30 mg) of artesunate were added into the GQD solution with vigorously stirring at different temperatures (−20, 24, 37 and 60 °C) for 30 min. Fourth, different amounts (X = 5, 10, 20 and 30 mg) of artesunate and mefloquine (1:1) were added into the GQD solution with vigorously stirring at different temperatures (−20, 24, 37 and 60 °C) for 60 min. Finally, 10 drops of chitosan solution (0.2 % w/w) were dropped on the optimal samples to stabilize the nanodrugs. And then, the 350 kDa molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) membranes were used for nanodrug purification using dialysis method.

All nanodrugs were used as baseline drugs against the parasite. The substances were dissolved in 60 % ethanol in PBS to obtain 10−2 mol/L concentration. This concentration was chosen due to its administration as a lethal dose in comparison with treatment in human malaria infection. Then the serial dilution of 10−3, 10−4, 10−5, 10−6 and 10−7 mol/L were prepared for relevant tests. To define sub lethal dose of drug, IC50 was determined.
The abbreviations of the names used in this study are presented in Table 1 .

To perform Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) a ThermoNicolet NEXUS 870 FTIR from Nicolet Instrument Corp., USA was used. The surface morphology of samples was investigated using a Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM Sigma, Zeiss Germany. Nanodrugs structure were confirmed by a transmission electron microscope (Zeiss-EM10C) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis were performed via X'Pert PRO MPD PANalytical Company, Netherland. Release study was investigated by an ultraviolet-visible spectrometer LABOMED, INC. UVD-3200, CA 90034, U.S.A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of artesunate and mefloquine IC50 on P. falciparum 3D7
ce:section.ce:para:
P. falciparum continuous culture was prepared as mentioned above. Lethal dose was rechecked and IC50 was determined. For preparing the IC50 of artesunate and mefloquine against the parasite, serial concentrations of artesunate and mefloquine were made as: 10−4, 10−5, 10−6, 10−7, 10−8, 10−9 and 10−10 mol/L, from stock solution (10−2 mol/L) in CCM under sterile condition and then were frozen in −20 °C until using time. Drug sensitivity test was conducted in 96-well microtitre plate in comparison with control groups. Each plate was designed as follow: RBCs control (negative control) including 10 μL fresh-washed O + RBC plus 190 μL of CCM. Positive control was charged with 10 μL infected RBC with 3.46 % parasitemia yield in continuous culture and 190 μL of CCM. Groups of 3 to 9 (groups Pi test): 10 μL of infected RBC (3.46 % parasitemia) plus 90 μL CCM and 100 μL drug dilution (from 10−4 mol/L concentration to 10−10 mol/L for each test respectively). Negative and positive control were respectively designed to check fresh red blood cells and parasite growth enhanced during the incubation. Plates were placed in the candle jar and kept in a 37 °C incubator for 24 h. A thin smear was made from sediment of each well after removing liquid culture media. Smears were stained with 5 % Giemsa stain in distilled water about 20 min. In the case of control and concentrations, average parasitemia were achieved based on counting infected RBCs against 10,000 RBC. The tests were repeated three times and mean of parasite percentage was calculated. Rate of parasitemia and inhibitory effect of each drug concentration on parasite growth was calculate as following and IC50 was achieved using SPSS software.
(1)
Inhibitory rate = 100 −
mean of parasitemia in each test group mean of parasitemia in control group
× 100

The release behavior of drugs from samples was studied using dialysis method by immersing the nanoparticles (1 mg), containing dialysis bag in 25 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4 at 37 °C) in the time range of 36 h. UV–Vis spectrometry method was used to determine the number of released drugs at the maximum wavelength of nanodrugs, 236 (Art) and 224 (Mef) nm.

The culture of P. falciparum was conducted at the National Malaria Laboratory, School of Public Health, Tehran University of Medical Sciences. The procedure was as follow: continues culture of P. falciparum 3D7 was performed according to the Trager and Jenson procedure with some modifications [20]. briefly, frozen sample of P. falciparum 3D7 was taken out of liquid nitrogen and thawed, then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 1 min, the supernatant was removed, and the sediment was washed twice with complete culture medium (RPMI 1640, human serum AB+: 10 %, hypoxanthine: 50 mg/L, gentamycin: 50 mg/L (CCM)). The pellet was dropped into a 25 mm in diameter plate containing CCM medium as well as 10 % of O+ human RBCs hematocrit, and Albumax (0.5 %) were added to each plate as a growth factor for parasites. Plates were placed in a candle jar and incubated at 37 °C. CCM was replaced with fresh medium every 48 h. Human RBC was added every week to maintain 10 % hematocrit. Thin blood smears were prepared from the sedimentation of each plate and then percentage of parasites was counted against 10,000 red blood cells. When the parasitemia reached to 10 %, GQD, GQD-Art, GQD-Mef, Chi, GQD-Art/Chi, GQD-Mef/Chi and GQD-Art-Mef/Chi were added to microplates. The first and final well of the microplate were negative and positive controls, respectively. The microplate was finally transferred to a candle jar, and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The tests were repeated in three microplate rows to confirm the results. Finally, the parasitemia was estimated by the microscopic and enzymatic (pfLDH) methods.

The pheochromocytoma of the rat adrenal medulla cell PC12 was obtained from the Iran University of Medical Science, Tehran, Iran. In vitro evaluation of toxicity of synthesized nanocomposites was tested by MTT assay [21,22]. Briefly, PC12 cell was seeded into 96-well microculture plates at appropriate densities, 100 μL/well, and was allowed to adhere for 24 h before sample addition. The different concentrations of nanodrugs were added to the cell, 100 μL/well and incubated for 48 h at 37 °C in a humidified 5 % CO2 incubator. Each sample was tested in triplicate wells. At the end of the exposure, 10 μL of 5 mg/mL MTT was added to each well and the plates were incubated for 3 h at 37 °C, then the plates were incubated for 10 min at 37 °C. The optical density (OD) was read on a plate reader at a wavelength of 570–630 nm. The growth inhibition rate was calculated by the following formula:
(2)
Growth inhibitory rate
%
=
OD
control − OD
treated
OD
control
× 100

The direct microscopic observation of blood specimens to detect the P. falciparum is still the gold standard for malaria diagnosis. Microscopic diagnosis of malaria was performed by staining thick and thin blood films on a glass slide to visualize the malaria parasite. Parasites were stained using Giemsa stain (5 %) [18].
Thin smears were prepared from each well and then stained with Giemsa stain to determine the percentage of parasites with microscopy.

The number of infected erythrocytes was counted against 10,000 infected and non-infected erythrocytes and the percentage of infection was determined. The mean was calculated and compared with the control group, then the percentage of growth inhibition was determined for each dilution. Statistical analysis was performed by SPSS software version V. 18.

Parasitemia was determined by measuring the pfLDH activity using spectrophotometry, according to a modified version of the method of Makler and Hinrichs [23,24]. In summary, 100 μL of Malstat reagent (0.11 % v/v Triton-100; 115.7 mM lithium l-lactate; 30.27 mM Tris; 0.62 mM 3-acetylpyridine adenine dinucleotide) with pH 9 and then 25 μL of PES/NBT (1.96 mM nitro blue tetrazolium chloride; 0.24 mM phenazine ethosulphate) were added to each well of a 96-well plate. Finally, 20 μL of a suspension culture medium containing 1 % parasitemia, 2 % hematocrit and different concentrations of the nanodrugs were added to the previous solution and the absorbance was then read at 650 nm. The first and last wells were contained the negative (blood without parasites) and positive controls (blood containing parasites), respectively. The percentage of parasitemia was calculated using the following formula:
(3)
Parasitemia
%
=
OD
test − OD
negative control
OD
positive control − OD
negative control
× 100

To purify the ring stage of P. falciparum (isolation of parasites), the test flask contents were centrifuged at 289 ×g for 5 min, the supernatant was removed, and parasites were isolated by adding 40 times the volume of 0.02 % saponin in PBS 1×. The solution was then kept on ice for 30 min before it was centrifuged at 4 °C for 20 min at 2057 ×g and washed three times with PBS 1×. The purified sedimented parasite was centrifuged at 25,200 ×g at 4 °C for 5 min and the supernatant was removed [25].

After evaluation of data and confirming their normal distribution, the data were statistically analyzed using SPSS V. 18 by one-way ANOVA, Student's t-test and nonlinear regression (sigmoidal dose-response model). P values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

The FESEM and TEM images of GQD are presented in Fig. 1A and B. The GQDs are well dispersed with a uniform size distribution, whose average sizes are about 6.53 nm (insets in Fig. 1). TEM images also show crystalline GQDs with a lattice measurement of 0.203 nm, which coincide with the graphene (002) plane [26]. The images with a hexagonal honeycomb structure demonstrate that the GQDs are nearly defect-free graphene single crystals [27].

Numerous literature reports signify the nanoparticles to be efficient peroral drug delivery systems. However, their efficient uptake is evidently charge and size-dependent with smaller nanoparticles generally exhibiting a better uptake and transport [28]. Smaller particles with a size of <500 nm easily traverse the epithelial cell membrane base on surface charge and by endocytosis. Also due to a high surface area to volume ratio, they exhibit an enhanced release of the nano drugs by surface erosion and diffusion simultaneously exhibiting an efficient penetration and permeation through the physiological drug barriers due to their smaller size [29,30]. The optimized nano drugs of GQD-Art-30/Chi (24°), GQD-Mef-10/Chi (60°), GQD-Mef-20/Chi (37°) and QGD-Art-Mef-10/Chi (1:1, 60°) exhibited a particle size range of 40–50, 70–80, 40–50 and 30–40, respectively. (Fig. 2B, D, F, H). In addition, the TEM images showed these small particles have traces of carbon, which confirms that GQDs binds to drugs surfaces and a drug well-loaded (dark points) on the bright quantum dot plane [26].

Art is a hydrophilic molecule (with hydroxyl groups) to the formulation of this compound as a nano-drug was performed by hydrogen bonding with GQD (we confirmed hydrogen bonding by FTIR). The connection of these two compounds formed nanospheres (Fig. 2A). Mef is a positively charged molecule. To formulate this active pharmaceutical ingredient as a nano-drug, the first step was to nanoprecipitate Mef with GQD, a negatively charged molecule [31,32]. The association of these two compounds formed rods nano-objects (Fig. 2C, E, G).

FTIR studies have been performed to determine the presence of functional groups and to confirm the successful synthesis of synthetic nano-drugs. The FTIR spectra of GQD, GQD-Art, GQD-Mef, Chi, GQD-Art/Chi, GQD-Mef/Chi and GQD-Art-Mef/Chi are presented in Fig. 3 and Table 2 . For GQD, the peak at 281 cm−1 was related to t stretching vibrations of COC and the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations were reflected at 2924 cm−1. Bands appeared at 1562 and 3453 cm−1 were an indication of s skeletal vibration of aromatic rings and stretching vibration of CH in aromatic rings and stretching vibration of hydroxyl group, respectively [33,34]. The peak observed at 1384 cm−1 was related to group CO (Alkoxy).

After adding Art, the appeared bands at 1383, 1558 and 2988 cm−1 were related to stretching vibration of CO, bending vibration of OH and CH, respectively, which can be an indication of hydrogen bonding between GQD and Art due to crystallization [15].

For GQD-Mef, the band at 3448 cm−1 was related to the overlapped stretching vibration of OH in Mef and GQD [35]. The appeared bands at 1601 and 1636 cm−1 were related to the CN in quinoline of Mef [36], it appeared as a sharp band in 1558 cm−1 due to interactions with GQD groups. The band in the 2925 cm−1 due to ʋ(NH2 +) is attributed to protonation of the starting material mefloquine at the piperidine N atom [37]. The absorption band at 1384 cm−1 due to ʋ(CF) in mefloquine retains its position and intensity in the spectra of the compounds [38].

For Chi sample, the peaks appeared at 1588 and 1709 cm−1 were related to amid (I) and amid (II), respectively. The band at 3355 cm−1 was related to the overlapped stretching vibration of OH and NH groups [39]. The bands at 452, 615, 888, 1015 and 2622 cm−1 were assigned to the out of plane bending vibrations of NH, out of plane bending vibrations of CO, out of plane bending vibrations of COC and stretching vibration in Chi, respectively.

For GQD-Art/Chi, the peaks appeared at range 2000, 2919 and 2800 cm−1 were related to the COO group, CH, CH2 and CH3 and the overlapped stretching vibration of OH and NH groups in Art, Chi and GQD, respectively. Also, a characteristic peak of the amino group (for Chi) was observed in 1560 cm−1, which is similar to that found in the Chi curve, which confirms the presence of chitosan on the nanoparticle surface [40].

For GQD-Mef/Chi sample, the band at 3448 cm−1 was related to the overlapped stretching vibration of OH groups in GQD, Mef and Chi. Furthermore, the band at 1559 cm−1 was related to the overlapped the CN in quinoline of Mef and amid (II) groups in Chi. The band in the 2925 cm−1 due to ʋ(NH2 +) is attributed to protonation of the starting material mefloquine, has also been observed in the QD-Mef/Chi spectrum. In addition, the CF group of mefloquine (1386 cm−1), similar to the QD-Mef spectrum, maintained its position and intensity.

For GQD-Art-Mef/Chi, the band at 1384 cm−1 was related to the overlapped stretching vibration of CO groups in Art and CF group of mefloquine. The peak in 1560 cm−1 is related to the hydrogen bonds of Art, Mef and GQD and also overlapped with the CN group of mefloquine. In addition, peak 3016 cm−1 belongs to the NH stretching group of Chi and the hydroxyl of Chi and Mef. The proposed mechanism for the interactions among components is shown in Fig. 4 .

XRD patterns of GQD, GQD-Art/Chi, GQD-Mef/Chi and GQD-Art-Mef/Chi are shown in Fig. 5 . The d-spacing of most intense peak was calculated by using Bragg's relation (Eq. 4).
(4)
nλ = 2 dsin θ
where n is an integer, λ is wavelength of X-ray for the copper target and θ is angle between the incident and reflected rays.

The d-spacing value for GQD from (002) plane at 29° was 0.13 nm. The large d-spacing value of the GQDs indicates that synthesized GQDs still contain oxygen containing functional groups. This could be related to the size and edge effects of GQDs. The broad nature of the diffraction peak is attributed to the nanoscale size of the GQDs with only few layers of graphene sheets [25]. The very broad peaks at around 13 and 28° is attributed to chitosan structure [40]. Also, a broad peak at 2θ = 14° was appeared in GQD-Art/Chi, which is attributed to the crystalline nature of Art and diffraction peak at 2θ = 28.9° were observed which is characteristic chitosan peak and overlapped with the characteristic GQD peak [41].

For GQD-Mef/Chi, diffraction peaks at 2θ = 11°, 2θ = 28° and 2θ = 42° for Mef were observed which is attributed to the crystalline nature of sample [42] and the increase of peak intensity at 2θ = 28° indicates the overlapped with the characteristic chitosan peak. Increasing the peak intensities at 2θ = 11°, 29°, 42° for the nanodrug indicates that the characteristic peaks of Chi, GQD, Art and Mef probably overlapped.

The obtained results from the release rate of the combined nanocomposite of artesunate and mefloquine at different times show the controlled release of these drugs so that 99 %, 96 %, and 90 % of the loaded GQD-Art/Chi, GQD-Mef/Chi, and GQD-Art-Mef/Chi were released after 36 h, respectively. Fig. 6 shows the release rate of the combined nanocomposite of artesunate and mefloquine at different times.
As the graph shows, the pattern of drug-release from nanoparticles follows the slow-release pattern.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT assay of GQD-Art-Mef/Chi in cell culture
ce:section.ce:para: MTT assay was used to evaluate the toxicity of synthesized nanocomposite (GQD-Art-Mef/Chi) in a cell culture medium. The results showed that the concentration of 100 μg of the combined nanocomposite drugs on GQD in this study could kill 50 % of cells. Therefore, the concentrations studied in this study include 2.5, 5, and 10 μg/mg without toxicity for the PC12 cell line (Fig. 7 ).

The viability effects showed that 100 μg/mL GQD-Art-Mef/Chi was perfectly nontoxic on the PC12 cells compared with the control group. The values are expressed as mean ± standard deviation from three independent experiments with P < 0.001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-Plasmodium effect of the combined nanocomposite artesunate and mefloquine on GQD
ce:section.ce:para: The results of the anti-parasitic effect of the combination of artesunate and mefloquine on P. falciparum in a culture medium were recorded by two microscopic and enzymatic methods (pfLDH) (Table 3 ). The results showed that the differences reported by microscopic and enzymatic methods were statistically significant (p < 0.05). Table 3 also shows the evaluation of the amount of IC50 and IC90 nanocomposites synthesized. Also, the analysis of the results of the anti-Plasmodium effects of nanoparticles synthesized in Table 3 showed that the combination of artesunate and mefloquine on GQD had a higher inhibitory effect on P. falciparum in culture medium than chloroquine and GQD alone. On the other hand, GQD alone had a little inhibitory effect on P. falciparum. The effect of artesunate combined with GQD was more than artesunate alone, which was a significant difference (p < 0.05). According to the results of Table 3, GQD-Art-Mef/Chi synthesized at a concentration of 3.6 μg/mL was able to inhibit the growth of 50 % of parasites in the culture medium (IC50 = 3.6 μg/mL). The results of IC50 and IC90 showed that at IC50 concentration, GQD-Art-Mef/Chi had a greater effect than all drugs against parasites. Also, at IC90 concentration, GQD-Art-Mef/Chi showed the greatest effect on P. falciparum.

Malaria is one of the most important parasitic infections in the world. More than 40 % of the world's population lives in malaria-endemic areas. Due to the emergence of P. falciparum strains resistant to chloroquine, mefloquine, and artesunate in recent decades, developing countries facing difficulties in treating acute cases of the disease. Artesunate is the most potent anti-malarial drug used to treat multidrug-resistant falciparum malaria. The cytotoxicity of artesunate has been mainly attributed to selective, irreversible damage and catalysis of iron by parasitic biomolecules. For this reason, it seems necessary to find new drugs with minimal side effects [43,44].

Studies have been performed to evaluate new anti-malarial compounds against P. falciparum. Combination anti-parasitic drug therapy and combined nanocomposite drugs with plant extracts are some of the new approaches of researchers to overcome more effectively against P. falciparum. The results of our study showed that the correlation drug GQD-Art-Mef/Chi had a higher inhibitory effect on P. falciparum in culture medium than chloroquine and GQD alone. Synthesized drug GQD-Art-Mef/Chi at a concentration of 3.6 μg/mL could inhibit the growth of 50 % of P. falciparum in culture medium (IC50 = 3.6 μg/mL).

Oyeyemi et al. in 2018 [17] showed that artesunate and mefloquine-artesunate have a greater anti-malarial effect when used with nano-carriers than when used alone. The synthesized nanocomposite at a dose of 5 mg/kg was able to reduce 79 % the parasitemia after five days treatment in mice infected with Plasmodium berghei, which was more effective than the positive control group [17]. In another study, the LC50 of GQD versus Anopheles stephensi was 0.157–6.323 ppm, which was different for the larval and adult stages. Also, IC50 of GQD was resistant and sensitive against P. falciparum strains, 82.43 μg/mL and 85.17 g/mL, respectively [45]. The results of these studies are consistent with the results of our study. They show that the association of anti-malarial drugs with nanoparticles and GQD can increase the effectiveness of anti-malarial drugs. Also, GQD is a suitable option for the treatment of P. falciparum.

On the other hand, one of the goals of our study was to introduce a new drug combination with fewer side effects and toxicity, which other researchers have studied. The results of Abedi et al.'s [46] study in 2016, which compared the effect of ethanolic extract and saffron fractions with chloroquine on P. berghei in mice, showed that concentrations of 700 and 350 mg/kg of saffron extract had the greatest effect in the treatment of sick mice. Also, in 2020, Hajialaiani et al. [47] conducted a study to investigate the effect of the active fraction of the venom of the Naja oxiana on the profile of malaria parasite metabolites. The results showed that the fraction with IC50 0.026 μg/mL and 63 kDa has the most effective fraction on P. falciparum and had a little hemolytic effect on red blood cells. Investigation of parasite metabolites in culture medium showed that the pathways of pyruvate cycle, Krebs cycle, and NAD were changed. The animal model study showed that the active fraction at the therapeutic dose of 2.5 mg/kg had an inhibitory effect on parasite growth in mice and metabolites l-histidine, l-cysteine, cholesterol sulfate, l-leucine, and isoleucine during treatment which were changed by snake venom. Considering P. berghei infected mice showed that the hydroalcoholic extract of ginger at a therapeutic dose of 250 mg/kg reduced parasitism by 62 %, which significantly differed from the control group.

The highest survival rate in mice was 250 mg/kg with a mean of 24 ± 2 days, which significantly differed from other treatment groups. Comparison of liver and kidney of mice with the negative control group showed that the hydroalcoholic extract of ginger was non-toxic to these organs. Following their 2020 research on the synthesized NDC-CQ nano composition, the researchers concluded that the average size of the synthesized nanoparticle was 239 nm. It also showed good solubility in water and had a slow release. The results of MTT and tissue pathology of their research showed that this nanoparticle had no toxic effect on PC12 cells and body tissues of mice. A concentration of 2.5 μg/mL of NDC-CQ in culture medium and a dose of 10 mg/kg in mice inhibited 82 % and 97 % of parasite growth, respectively. Due to the better effect of NDC-CQ than chloroquine on the Plasmodium, this compound can be considered as an effective anti-Plasmodium drug that recommended more comprehensive research in this field [48]. Examining the reaction of graphene oxide on Plasmodium showed that graphene oxide at different concentrations could significantly prevent the invasion of malaria parasites into red blood cells. Graphene oxide also delayed the conversion of the ring phase to parasite trophozoites. Therefore, nanomaterial-based strategies can be used to combat malaria [49]. The above studies showed that different biomaterials based on nanomaterials, in combination with anti-parasitic drugs and even alone can have anti-Plasmodium properties, which are consistent with the results of our study.

The study showed that the percentage of continuous suppression of Plasmodium in nano-curcumin-artesunate-d,l-lactic-co-glycolic acid at a concentration of 5 mg/kg on day 5 was 79 % and on day 8 was 72.5 %. Alanine aminotransferase and aspartate aminotransferase were significantly lower in mice exposed to 5 and 10 mg/kg nano-curcumin-artesunate-PLGA compared with the negative control group. The use of artesunate correlation with nanoparticles in the mentioned studies showed promising effects against Plasmodium that these results are consistent with the results obtained in the present study [17].

Also, the results obtained from the present study showed that the combination of two drugs, mefloquine-artesunate, had a lower IC50 and was more effective than the single use of each of these two drugs. Phunuch Muhamad [50] has cited, the IC50 of artesunate and mefloquine was significantly higher in patients with LPF than ACPR and re-infection. The proportion of isolates with reduced sensitivity to artesunate or mefloquine in the LPF group was significantly higher than the ACPR group. A weak but statistically significant correlation (p = 0.01) was observed between IC50 of artesunate and parasitic clearance time (PCT). There was no significant relationship between in vitro susceptibility of parasitic isolates to chloroquine or quinine and clinical response. In vitro susceptibility of P. falciparum isolates to artesunate and mefloquine may be used as a reliable drug to predict the clinical-pathological response after three days of combination therapy. In a study by Hoglund et al. [51], weight, sex, acceptance of parasites, and molecular markers of mefloquine resistance did not significantly affect mefloquine pharmacokinetics. There was a significant difference in maximum concentration (Cmax) and elimination half-life (t1/2) in patients who failed treatment compared to patients with successful treatment. This study described the success of the 2-day treatment of mefloquine as part of the 3-day treatment of mefloquine-artesunate in patients with uncomplicated P. falciparum from Thailand. The above studies showed that the combined use of mefloquine-artesunate has significant anti-malarial effects on falciparum, which are consistent with the results of our study. Overall, the results of this study could suggest a new correlated nanocomposite with a significant impact on the treatment of malaria in the research phase. This correlated compound was more effective and also reduced the use of mefloquine and artesunate, which resulted in fewer side effects for patients. Mefloquine had a synergistic effect on artesunate and increased its effectiveness. Also, according to the observations and the obtained results, the solubility of the drug mefloquine increased, which, after systematic research in this field, will probably reduce the side effects of this drug. The use of this correlated drug in the clinical phase requires further research.

In this study, graphene quantum dot-based antimalarial nano-drug delivery systems were prepared on Plasmodium falciparum. The drugs release was controlled by the addition of graphene quantum dots. The equilibrium releasing time was 33 h. FTIR results indicated on generating of hydrogen bonds between the graphene quantum dot and drugs. The prepared nanodrugs had an IC50s of 9.2, 18.6 and 3.6 μg/mL for GQD-Art/Chi, GQD-Mef/Chi and GQD-Art-Mef/Chi, respectively. The result of MTT analysis indicated on non-toxic nature of the nano-drugs. While excipients are generally used for the fabrication of nano-sized drug formulation, the present approach offers an excipient free nano-formulation. Moreover, the transformation of parent drug molecules to nano-size dimensions can result in their high uptake and controlled release. We envisage that fabricated nano-assemblage has taken us one step closer to solving the growing demand of an active, less toxic substitute.
